paragraphs.body: The old people told stories with their heart. Everyone dancing in the street. To live in Louisiana means to be yourself And show how we live everything out To the fullest. To live in Louisiana, we show that we speak Our culture we speak it like no other So, when we say noo Brudda we speak And use that with others. I was born and raised in Shreveport La it really isn’t nothing down there for real, just a regular city. I was trying to get out of Shreveport because it is nothing good there for me. The good things that I left were my dad and friends and my old high school teammates. I was excited to come to Pittsburgh and experience the new change that was coming. Being here now makes me miss home even more than ever, even though I couldn't wait to leave and get out of Shreveport before I got to Pittsburgh. Now I just keep thinking about home.

paragraphs.body: My ‘home’ language is the language I speak with my friends, and cousins. Some of the things we say are “my lill yeah,” “boyy,” and “slut ahh boy.” We use the word “chill” when we feel somebody being annoying, but I think everyone uses chill, and we use “flonky” and like “wassam” yu can use “wassam” for like speaking to somebody or like Finna fight someone like “wassam” do some. For example, when I was at my game this dude almost hit me in my face, I got mad and i was like “wassam” like do some, I was hot, and I had to take a play of because I almost got kicked out of the game.

annotations.comment: great concrete and specific details and examples!
annotations.context_right: when we feel somebody being annoying, but I think everyone uses chill, and we use

paragraphs.body: “Brudda” and “Donkey” are the words I speak with my friends and family. It is a language of just what we created and what we thought was funny and we just started saying it and stuff for example we could be chillin or calling someone we be like, “wassup brudda.” Another example is like if one of my friends do something dumb, we will say to that person, “Donkey of the day.” We use different words, but some people cannot understand it. This shows how special we are because not everyone can express how we feel except you from that boot. The boot is my home, and this is my community and where I feel comfortable.

annotations.text: feel except you from that boot.
annotations.comment: Nice. Your voice is so good!

paragraphs.body: I didn't realize how valuable my home language was until I came to college in Pittsburgh. One day I was out to get food and when I went I went to Canes on up behind my apartment building and when I went to order the cashier could not understand me and the words that I was using, and It kind of made me feel awkward because of his face expression when I'm talking he is so confuse about the situation. If I was in my hometown, I would not have any problem with anyone struggling to understand what I am talking about. I was walking the street. I was with my Brudda. I seen this girl. I said to ha wassam luv. She said wassam back. Using my home language is another thing back home because everyone knows how everything is and the slang that we use across the whole state of Louisiana. Every sense I’ve moved here to Pittsburgh and meeting other people from other states and I’ve learned that other people have different languages and stuff, I have a roommate he is from Pittsburgh and they slang that he be using is crazy I always be like what does that mean and I be looking at him crazy because I never heard of what he is saying, and that’s the same for when I am around all of his friends and stuff they talk just like him. They use words like Nefs, Homiez, on Steve’s grave, 100it, words like this he say every single day and I also have another roommate that and his name is Jaremiah and he is from Florida and he has a different kind of accent as well and we kinda act the same we are both from the south, but he still have a different slangs that he using such as, Jit, woah, when they say back they say bike I do not know why but he mostly uses jit. The word jit, you can use the word jit in so as many ways such as like the word we use wassam, like calling someone or like doing something with that person. Even though all 3 of us are from different areas we are not always able to understand each other but we never had problems communicating. Even though others outside of those 3 people cannot really understand me sometimes when I talk, it makes me sometimes feel that I cannot open my mouth and speak because others do not really understand me.

annotations.text: it makes me sometimes feel that I cannot open my mouth and speak because others do not really understand me.
annotations.comment: interesting. I wonder if this has impact on other parts of your life?

paragraphs.body: Before I came to Pittsburgh I was in Louisiana and I would travel around the world and go to my games and stuff, but I never really experienced going out or anything like that. I came on a visit to Pittsburgh before I actually moved in here, and I really didn't get to see the city like that, so everything was really unknown to me when I got here. When I came up here to Pittsburgh everything was different the streets, the people, you would see more people on the side of the road walking, and more homeless people than usual. Just everything around me is a big adjustment to school and how the teachers teach them how they teach back home but I am getting used to the adjustment and I’m starting to figure out the slang that people use down here.

Owen took me to his little brother high school football game, he plays for Oakland catholic and when I went as soon as I walked in the stadium I seen how everything so different from where I am from like how the coaches was calling plays and coaching on the sideline was very different from what my old high school coach’s did for me and my old teammates from back home. The coach’s had crazy slang and different accents to me I really couldn’t understand what they were saying, and this for me now at Pitt with my current coaches me getting to learn from my new coach’s and how they coach others and adapting to the new environment that I am in right now. For example, when I was back at home with my old high school team my old coach’s would scream at everything you do and I went to a private school so after everything we would pray and things like that, and that’s something for me as well that I’m getting used to as well when I was back home at my old school we had bible study every day at 6:45 in the morning for 30 min and then go to practice I was literally in church every day and that’s something I don’t have out here, that culture that had back home then now me being out here.

Even though I am not down south in Louisiana, and I do not have my home, and my home language, and all my friends and family with me, I am here now in Pittsburgh. I have been blessed by God to have the ability to adapt to being up north and discovering different people, and places and other things, and other slang that people use. Now that I am in college I will be meeting people all over the word and the language that they speak will be very interesting and I’m sure when they hear my southern accent and the slang that I speak to them during our conversation they will be amused and ready to hear what I have to say when I am talking to them.

I'm from Shreveport and sometimes I do feel different and left out. I feel left out because most people don't understand me and can't communicate with them how I can with someone that from down south. But I'm proud at the same time being from Shreveport that I am changing as a young adult and adapting to the new. Having my home language reminds me I'm close to home even if I'm not physically there.

annotations.text: I'm from Shreveport and sometimes I do feel different and left out. I
